Arguments.prompt: Download US state and county boundary shapefiles from the Census Bureau. Use these exact URLs: states shapefile at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip and counties shapefile at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip. Download each ZIP file, extract its contents, and load the shapefiles using geopandas. From the states shapefile, filter to the single row where the NAME column equals 'Oregon'. From the counties shapefile, filter to the single row where the NAME column equals 'Benton' and the STATEFP code matches Oregon's STATEFP value (which is '41'). Compute the bounding box of Benton County by taking its geometry's total_bounds (minx, miny, maxx, maxy), then expand each side by 0.5 degrees: subtract 0.5 from minx and miny, add 0.5 to maxx and maxy. Create a rectangular polygon from these expanded bounds using shapely.geometry.box and store it as a GeoDataFrame with CRS EPSG:4326. Download the NOAA GHCND station inventory from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/ghcn/daily/ghcnd-inventory.txt. This file is fixed-width with columns: STATION (chars 1-11), LATITUDE (chars 13-20), LONGITUDE (chars 22-30), ELEMENT (chars 32-35), FIRSTYEAR (chars 37-40), LASTYEAR (chars 42-45). Parse it accordingly. Keep only rows where ELEMENT is 'TMAX' or 'TMIN' and where LASTYEAR is greater than or equal to the current calendar year. For each station that appears in both TMAX and TMIN filtered rows, keep it only once. Filter stations to those whose LATITUDE and LONGITUDE fall within the expanded bounding box (minx <= LONGITUDE <= maxx and miny <= LATITUDE <= maxy). Create a GeoDataFrame from these stations with point geometries using LONGITUDE and LATITUDE columns and CRS EPSG:4326. Save the following four files: State.geojson (Oregon state boundary), County.geojson (Benton County boundary), OuterBoundary.geojson (expanded bounding box polygon), and Stations.geojson (filtered station points with STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE columns). Upload all four files to the S3 data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot. The file paths in the data store should be WeatherGeographicPlot/State.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/County.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/OuterBoundary.geojson, and WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson.

Download the file WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson from the S3 data store. Load it as a GeoDataFrame using geopandas. It contains columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, and geometry. Determine the target week as follows: take today's date (use Python's datetime.date.today()), subtract 28 days, then find the most recent Monday on or before that date by subtracting (weekday()) days where weekday() returns 0 for Monday. That Monday is the week_start. The week_end is week_start plus 6 days (the following Sunday). For each station in the GeoDataFrame, download its full daily CSV record from the URL https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/<STATION_ID>.csv where <STATION_ID> is the value in the STATION column. The CSV has a header row. The relevant columns are DATE (in YYYY-MM-DD format), TMIN, and TMAX. Filter the CSV rows to only those where DATE falls between week_start and week_end inclusive. Discard any rows where TMIN or TMAX is missing or NaN. If a station has no valid rows after filtering, skip it entirely. For stations with valid rows, compute the mean of TMIN and the mean of TMAX across all remaining rows, then divide each mean by 10.0 to convert from tenths of a degree Celsius to whole degrees Celsius. Collect all qualifying stations into a single GeoDataFrame with columns: STATION (string), geometry (point geometry from original Stations.geojson), TMIN (float, averaged and converted), TMAX (float, averaged and converted). Save this GeoDataFrame as TemperatureData.geojson and upload it to the S3 data store at path WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureData.geojson. Also save a plain text file named WeekRange.txt containing two lines: the first line is week_start in YYYY-MM-DD format and the second line is week_end in YYYY-MM-DD format. Upload WeekRange.txt to the S3 data store at path WeatherGeographicPlot/WeekRange.txt.

Download the following files from the S3 data store: WeatherGeographicPlot/OuterBoundary.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureData.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/State.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/County.geojson, and WeatherGeographicPlot/WeekRange.txt. Load each GeoJSON file using geopandas. Load WeekRange.txt and read the first line as week_start (a date string in YYYY-MM-DD format) and the second line as week_end (a date string in YYYY-MM-DD format). Parse week_start into a Python date object. From OuterBoundary.geojson, extract the bounding box using total_bounds to get (minx, miny, maxx, maxy) representing the expanded bounding box around Benton County. From TemperatureData.geojson, extract station point coordinates (longitude = geometry.x, latitude = geometry.y), TMIN values, and TMAX values as numpy arrays. Create a 100x100 interpolation grid: use numpy.linspace(minx, maxx, 100) for the x-axis (longitude) and numpy.linspace(miny, maxy, 100) for the y-axis (latitude), then use numpy.meshgrid to create grid_x and grid_y arrays. Use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='cubic' to interpolate TMIN values from station points onto the grid, and separately interpolate TMAX values onto the grid. Create a matplotlib figure with two side-by-side subplots using figsize=(16, 7). For the left subplot (TMIN): use matplotlib's contourf with 20 levels and colormap 'Blues_r' and alpha=0.8 to render the interpolated TMIN grid. Overlay station scatter points using plt.scatter with the station longitude and latitude, colored by their TMIN values using the same 'Blues_r' colormap, with black edgecolors and zorder=5. Draw the Oregon state boundary from State.geojson using plot with color='black', linewidth=1.5, and no fill. Draw the Benton County boundary from County.geojson using plot with color='black', linewidth=2.0, and no fill. Set x-axis limits to (minx, maxx) and y-axis limits to (miny, maxy). Set x-ticks and y-ticks at intervals of 0.5 degrees within those ranges. Label x-axis as 'Longitude' and y-axis as 'Latitude'. Add a colorbar labeled 'Temperature (C)'. Title this subplot 'Minimum Temperature Heatmap (C)'. For the right subplot (TMAX): use contourf with 20 levels and colormap 'Reds' and alpha=0.8 to render the interpolated TMAX grid. Overlay station scatter points colored by TMAX using the 'Reds' colormap with black edgecolors and zorder=5. Draw the Oregon state boundary and Benton County boundary the same way as the left subplot. Set the same axis limits, tick intervals, and axis labels. Add a colorbar labeled 'Temperature (C)'. Title this subplot 'Maximum Temperature Heatmap (C)'. Format the figure supertitle as 'Temperature Heatmap for Benton County for week starting <weekday>, <month> <day>, <year>' where weekday is the full day name (e.g. Monday), month is the full month name (e.g. January), day is the zero-padded day of month, and year is the four-digit year, all derived from week_start. Use plt.tight_layout() before saving. Save the figure as TemperatureHeatmap.png using dpi=150. Upload TemperatureHeatmap.png to the S3 data store at path WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureHeatmap.png.
